transform conquer 

Transform-and-Conquer 
 
 

Representation Change 
 2-3 Trees 
Allow more than one key in the same node. 
a. 2-node contains a single key K and has two children (left and right). 
b. 3-node contains two ordered keys K1  and K2 (K1 < K2) and has 3 children. 
All leaves must be on the same level.  
A 2-3 tree is always perfectly height-balanced: the 
length of a path from the root to a leaf is the same for 
every leaf.  

Example 
Construction of a 2-3 tree for the list  9, 5, 8, 3, 2, 4, 7. 

Example 
Construction of a 2-3 tree for the list  9, 5, 8, 3, 2, 4, 7.

Space and Time Tradeoffs Concepts
moeljono.widjaja@umn.ac.id UMN IF431 Even Semester 2025-2026 6 / 12
Space and Time Tradeoffs 
Interplay between time and space in algorithm design  
 Input Enhancement – preprocess the input (or its part) to store some info to be 
used later in solving the problem. 
a. Sorting by counting 
b. Horspool and Boyer-Moore Algorithm 
 Prestructuring – preprocess the input to make accessing its element easier. 
a. Hashing 
b. Indexing schemes (B-trees) 
 

Space and Time Tradeoffs Concepts Input Enhancement: Sorting by Counting Input Enhancement: String Matching Prestructuring: Hashing Exercises
Input Enhancement: Sorting by Counting
moeljono.widjaja@umn.ac.id UMN IF431 Even Semester 2025-2026 7 / 12
Comparison-Counting Sort: Example 
To count, for each element of a list to be sorted, the total number of elements smaller than this element and record 
the results in a table. These numbers will indicate the positions of the elements in the sorted list: e.g., if the count is 
10 for some element, it should be in the 10th position (if we start counting with 0) in the sorted array 

Comparison-Counting Sort 

Comparison-Counting Sort 
 The number of comparisons: 
 
 
 
 The algorithm makes the same number of key comparisons as selection 
sort  
 In addition, uses a linear amount of extra space 
 On the positive side, the algorithm makes the minimum number of key 
moves, placing each of them directly in their final position in a sorted array 
 
 This algorithm works productively in a situation in which elements to be 
sorted belong to a known small set of values

Horspool’s Algorithm 
A simplified version of Boyer-Moore algorithm: 
 Preprocesses pattern to generate a shift table that determines how 
much to shift the pattern when a mismatch occurs. 
 Always makes a shift based on the text’s character c aligned with the 
last character in the pattern according to the shift table’s entry for c. 
 
Extra space 

Shift Table 
 
 We can precompute shift sizes and store them in a table. The table will be 
indexed by all possible characters that can be encountered in a text, including, 
for natural language texts, the space, punctuation symbols, and other special 
characters.  The table’s entries will indicate the shift sizes computed by the 
formula 
   
 
  

Shift Table 

Example 
 
 Shift table for BARBER in a text that comprises English letters 
and spaces (denoted by underscores).

Boyer-Moore Algorithm 
Based on same two ideas: 
1. Comparing pattern characters to text from right to left 
2. Precomputing shift sizes in two tables 
 bad-symbol table t(c) (same as Horspool table), but in use 
 Shift uses text character at mismatch (not the last one as Horspool’s) 
 Shift uses length of matching suffix 
 good-suffix table  d2 indicates how much to shift based on matched 
part (suffix) of the pattern. 
 Shift the pattern to the right by the number of positions computed by the 
formula: 
   
     

Boyer-Moore Algorithm 
Bad-symbol Shift 
 For Case 1 and 2, Boyer-Moore acts like Horspool’s. 
 Otherwise, if the rightmost character of the pattern does match, Boyer-
Moore compares preceding characters right to left until either all pattern’s 
characters match or a mismatch on text’s character c  is encountered after 
k > 0 matches. 
 
 
 bad-symbol shift  d1 = max{t1(c ) - k, 1}  
 
 
S: S0 . . . . . S E R . . . . . . . Sn-1 
P:       B A R B E R                 

Boyer-Moore Algorithm 
Good-suffix Shift 
 Good-suffix shift d2 is applied after 0 < k < m last characters 
were matched. 
 d2(k) = the distance between matched suffix of size k and its 
rightmost occurrence in the pattern that is not preceded by the 
same character as the suffix.

d2 = 5

d1 = t1(_) - 1 = 5 
d2 = 2 
d = max(5,2) = 5

Prestructuring: Hashing
moeljono.widjaja@umn.ac.id UMN IF431 Even Semester 2025-2026 9 / 12
Prestructuring 
 Linear search time in array is O(n) 
 Binary search time in array is O(log n) 
 Ideally, we want constant O(1) in the average case   Direct Access 
 Direct Address Table is a data structure that has the capability of mapping 
records to their corresponding keys using arrays.  
 In direct address tables, records are placed using their key values directly as 
indexes.  
 Prestructuring  Hashing (trade-off space vs time) 
 They facilitate fast searching, insertion and deletion operations. 

Hashing 
 
 Hashing is based on the idea of distributing keys among a one-
dimensional array H[0..m−1] called a hash table. The distribution is 
done by computing, for each of the keys, the value of some predefined 
function h called the hash function. 
 This function assigns an integer between 0 and m−1, called the hash 
address, to a key.

pending_tasks: Explain the concept of Hashing as a prestructuring strategy, including the purpose of a hash function and at least two common methods used to handle collisions when keys map to the same index.

completed_tasks.task: Define the general principle of Space and Time Trade-offs and explain the difference between the 'Input Enhancement' and 'Prestructuring' techniques with one example for each.
completed_tasks.answer: input enhancement is something like presorting, it works by parsing the input to be used for another time. Prestructuring works by making it easier to access, e.g. hashing or trees

completed_tasks.task: Explain the mechanism of Comparison-Counting Sort: how does the algorithm determine the final position of an element, and what is the specific trade-off involving extra space?
completed_tasks.answer: They do it by counting the amount of smaller and bigger elements. The tradeoff is it is slow but takes less space

completed_tasks.task: Describe the logic behind Horspool’s Algorithm for string matching, specifically how the 'Shift Table' is precomputed and used when a mismatch occurs.
Compare the Boyer-Moore Algorithm to Horspool’s, highlighting the two specific rules (bad-symbol and good-suffix) Boyer-Moore uses to determine the maximum shift distance.
